content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 19, 2017

David Mitrani, Counsel
International Association of Firefighters
FIREPAC Non-Federal (MPO)
Sandler Reiff Lamb
Rosenstein & Birkenstock, P.C.
1025 Vermont Ave NW, Suite 300
Washington, DC 20005

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-119

Dear Mr. Mitrani:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding campaign reporting provisions of
the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

QUESTION

May Mr. Edward Kelly, a committee treasurer for a state general purpose committee, sign
campaign finance reporting forms required by the Act with a digital signature?

CONCLUSION

The Commission encourages the modernization of campaign reporting and is hopeful that
electronic filing will be available soon for all campaign committees. Moreover, for purposes of state
filings submitted to the Secretary of State, the Commission also recognizes that the Secretary of
State's Office has particular expertise regarding digital signatures and approving companies that
certify digital signature technology. Accordingly, the Commission does not object to the Secretary
of State's Office accepting digital signatures on campaign finance reports, provided the digital
signatures meet the requirements of Government Code Section 16.5 and any applicable regulations
governing digital signatures adopted by the Secretary of State.

However, the Commission is not in the position to determine whether the Secretary of State's
Office has the personnel or capabilities to accept digital signatures on campaign finance reports.
Accordingly, we must defer your question to the Secretary of State's Office, and you should contact
the Secretary of State's Office for further guidance.

________________________
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-119
Page No. 2

FACTS

You request advice on behalf of the International Association of Firefighters - FIREPAC
Non-Federal Multipurpose Organization ("FIREPAC"), ID # 1255870, a California General
Purpose Committee, and Mr. Edward Kelly, in his capacity as the Treasurer of FIREPAC.
FIREPAC is a 527 organization registered with the Internal Revenue Service, which has registered
as a Multipurpose Organization with the Commission to participate in California elections.
Mr. Kelly also serves as the International Association of Firefighters' General Secretary-Treasurer,
a position that requires a great deal of travel on behalf of the union.

In light of the Commission's long-standing requirement that most campaign reports must be
signed with an original or "wet" signature, you have stated that the difficulty in obtaining an
original or "wet" signature may ultimately force Mr. Kelly to resign as the committee treasurer.
Accordingly, you ask whether it would be permissible for Mr. Kelly to sign the paper versions of
campaign finance reporting forms required under the Act with a digital signature.

ANALYSIS

The Act requires that "All reports and statements filed under this title shall be signed under
penalty of perjury and verified by the filer." (Section 81004(a).) The Act also requires any general
purpose committee that receives contributions or makes expenditures of $25,000 or more to file
electronically and that electronic filers do so under the penalty of perjury. (Section 84605(a)(2) and
(e).) The California Secretary of State administers Cal-Access, which allows filers to complete and
file reports electronically under Section 84605.

Notwithstanding the electronic filing of campaign finance reports, Section 84605(f) states
that the "paper copy shall continue to be the official filing for audit and other legal purposes." Thus,
after electronically filing reports through Cal-Access, campaign finance reports must be printed,
signed by the Treasurer, and mailed to the Secretary of State. The mailing date is considered the
date of the filing, and not the electronic filing date.

Undoubtedly, the historical purpose of requiring original paper filings was to ensure that the
signatories were fully responsible for the reports filed. Accordingly, the Commission has long
maintained that original paper filings must contain an original or "wet" signature. However, it
would be imprudent not to consider contemporary and widely accepted practices in interpreting the
provisions of the Act.

As you have pointed out, the Secretary of State's Office has particular expertise in the use of
digital signatures and the approval of companies to certify digital signature technology. Moreover,
Government Code Section 16.5 establishes that public agencies may choose to permit the use of
digital signatures and that the digital signature shall have the "same force and effect" as a manual
signature so long as it meets the requirements of the statute and any applicable regulations
governing digital signatures adopted by the Secretary State. Considering the Secretary of State's
unique expertise, the Commission is not opposed to the Secretary of State's Office permitting the
use of digital signatures on campaign finance reports at its discretion.

File No. A-17-119
Page No. 3

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Jack Woodside
General Counsel

By: Brian G. Lau
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

electronic filing will be available soon for all campaign committees. Moreover, for purposes of state 
filings submitted to the Secretary of State, the Commission also recognizes that the Secretary of
